Logical topology
This is how a customer's router gets its IP address from Origin. When the customer sets their router (or modem) to Dynamic IP — also shown as DHCP or "Obtain an IP address automatically" — it asks Origin's network for an address instead of using a fixed one. Below is how DHCP works: a quick four-step handshake called DORA — Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge. Open the router admin, choose Dynamic IP and Save to watch it run.

The basics — for everyday calls

Nine quick lessons covering what comes up on everyday calls. Open one — each gets its own focused page.

0 of 9 complete

1 What is DHCP?

Every device that goes on a network needs an IP address — think of it as a street address so data knows where to be delivered. DHCP (Dynamic Host Configuration Protocol) is the automatic system that hands those addresses out, so nobody has to type one into every device by hand.

When a customer's router connects, it has a quick four-step conversation with our DHCP server to get its address. That conversation is DORA: Discover → Offer → Request → Acknowledge. The router also receives the gateway (its way out to the internet) and DNS (the phone book that turns names like google.com into numbers). The Simulator tab shows this happening step by step.

2 What is DNS?

Websites have names (like google.com), but computers actually find each other using numbers — IP addresses. DNS (Domain Name System) is the internet's phone book: you give it a name, it gives back the number, and the connection can be made. It happens silently every time someone opens a website or app.

The modem normally gets its DNS server automatically as part of DHCP — it's one of the details handed over during the DORA handshake, alongside the IP address and gateway.

Why this matters on a call: if a customer says “I'm connected but no websites will load,” that's the classic DNS sign — the line itself is up, but the name look-ups are failing. Quick checks: try a different website (and the same site on another device), and look at the DNS settings on the modem; sometimes switching to a public DNS like 8.8.8.8 or rebooting clears it. On the Tech tab, this is the Layer 7 symptom in the OSI troubleshooting.

3 Why set the customer to “Dynamic IP (DHCP)”?

For nearly all home customers, our network is built to hand the router its address automatically. Dynamic IP simply means “let the router get its address from us via DHCP.” It's the normal, expected setting: it lets us manage a shared pool of addresses, swap equipment, and renew leases without anyone touching the customer's router.

Why this matters on a call: if a router is accidentally set to Static, it won't match what our network expects and the customer won't get online. Switching it back to Dynamic / DHCP is the fix for a lot of “no internet” cases — exactly the scenario you can demo on the Simulator tab.

4 Where does RADIUS fit?

Before a customer is allowed an address, the network usually checks “is this a real, active customer, and what plan are they on?” That check is RADIUS, which handles AAA — Authentication, Authorisation and Accounting:

Authentication — are you really our customer? Authorisation — which speed/features do you get? Accounting — log when you came online & for how long

So when the line comes up, the access equipment asks RADIUS to approve the session and return the customer's profile; DHCP then provides the actual IP address. The Accounting part is what produces the “Online for X days” and session details you saw in the back-office view on the Simulator tab. In short: RADIUS decides who they are and what they get; DHCP gives them the address.

5 CGNAT vs public / static IP

There aren't enough public IPv4 addresses in the world for everyone, so providers often place many customers behind one shared public address using CGNAT (Carrier-Grade NAT). For everyday use — browsing, streaming, apps, video calls — this works perfectly and the customer never notices.

Simplified diagram for training. Addresses shown are examples (the 100.x range we use in the sim is the kind of space typically seen with CGNAT).

The catch with CGNAT is the incoming direction. Because many customers share one address and sit behind the carrier's NAT, anything that needs the outside world to start a connection into the customer can fail.

Why a customer might ask for a static IP or to “opt out of CGNAT”: they're trying to run something that needs to be reachable from outside — port forwarding, a security camera/CCTV system, hosting a game server, remote access, or certain business VPNs. The fix is to give them a public dynamic IP or a static IP, or to remove them from CGNAT, so inbound connections can get through. A plain static address also stays the same over time, which is handy for businesses pointing services at it.
Important When a customer requests this, always get clear information on why they need to opt out, and suggest purchasing a Static IP. If they insist on a dynamic public IP, raise it to #help-telco-support with your reasoning for SME approval.

6 Bridge tap

A bridge tap is an extra, dead-end piece of phone wiring inside the property — often an old, unused phone point or extension still connected to the line. On a copper-based service it can act like a side-branch that goes nowhere: the signal runs down it and reflects back, which can affect the connection (slower speeds, dropouts, or trouble holding sync). On an NBN FTTN service the connection comes through to the first wall socket; the wiring inside the home isn't part of the network we manage, so we'd suggest the customer have a registered cabler look at it rather than trying to fix it from our end.

The wanted signal travels along the main line to the modem. Where a bridge tap branches off, some signal runs down the dead end, bounces back, and interferes with the line — causing slower speeds or dropouts.
When a line test indicates a possible bridge tap (e.g. on SHS): let the customer know it relates to in-home wiring, and use the guide below to explain what it is and what needs to happen. This is general guidance, not a legal statement — always follow your team's current approved wording and process.
Guide — helping the customer understand a bridge tap

What it is: Explain that the line test is indicating a “bridge tap” — an extra piece of older phone wiring inside their property (often an unused phone point or an old extension) that's still connected to the line. A simple way to picture it: it's like a dead-end branch off a road — the signal travels down that wiring and reflects back, which can affect the connection and is a common contributor to slower speeds or dropouts.

What needs to happen to fix it: Let them know that on their type of connection the service comes through to the first wall socket, and the wiring inside the home isn't part of the network we look after — so it isn't something we're able to work on from our side. Suggest they arrange a registered cabler to take a look and remove that extra wiring if needed.

Setting expectations: Removing it can give the line a cleaner, more direct path, which may improve stability and speed — but don't promise a specific result. Any cabler's visit or costs are arranged directly by the customer. Note on the account that a bridge tap was identified.

7 Slow sync vs slow speed

Sync rate (the line rate) is the speed the modem locks onto the line — the ceiling for the connection, set by the line type and, on copper, the distance to the node. Speed (throughput) is how fast data actually moves when browsing or downloading; it's always at or below the sync rate and is also affected by Wi-Fi, the device, congestion and the website. So slow sync = the line itself can't go faster (a capability limit); slow speed with a healthy sync usually means Wi-Fi / device / congestion / DNS — fix that rather than changing the plan.

If the sync is healthy but it still feels slow, the cause is usually between the modem and the user, not the line. Quick checks:

  • Wi-Fi — distance from the router, walls, interference or an old router. Test on an Ethernet cable to rule Wi-Fi in or out — the single most useful check.
  • Peak-hour congestion — the nbn network is shared, so speeds can dip in the busy evening window (~7–11pm). Compare a speed test at peak vs off-peak.
  • Too many devices / heavy use at once — 4K streaming, large downloads or cloud backups all share the one connection.
  • The device, browser or one website — try another device or site, clear the cache, and check DNS.
  • In-home wiring or an unsuitable modem — faulty sockets or cables, a bridge tap, or a modem that doesn't match the plan.

Different NBN types reach different sync rates — FTTP and HFC can hit the top tiers, FTTC is usually high, and FTTN/B depends on the copper distance, so it's most often where the line can't reach the plan's speed.

The plan's tier is higher than the line's attainable rate, so the connection can't deliver it — that gap is what the speed-comms email explains.
On an SHS you'll see three line rates: Actual — the modem's current sync to the node; Attainable — the most the line can sync under current conditions (the real ceiling); Assured — NBN's minimum guaranteed sync, used to decide if a fault can be raised.
On the comms call If a line's attainable rate is below the plan's tier, the line physically can't deliver the plan — that's the speed email the customer got. Options to talk through: move to a lower plan, check fibre-upgrade eligibility, or stay on the plan knowing the limit. The limit comes from the NBN line, not Origin, so it would affect any provider.

8 Wi-Fi bands: 2.4 GHz, 5 GHz & band steering

Wi-Fi is the wireless signal the router broadcasts inside the home. Modern routers send it on two bands — 2.4 GHz and 5 GHz. Knowing the difference is how we fix most "Wi-Fi keeps dropping out" and "slow over Wi-Fi" calls.

Both bands carry the same internet — they're just two different "lanes" with a trade-off between reach and speed:

2.4 GHz reaches the far rooms and through walls but is slower; 5 GHz is much faster but fades over distance and through walls. Simplified for training.
2.4 GHz5 GHz
Range / coverageReaches further; better through walls & floorsShorter; struggles through walls
SpeedSlower (typically ~50–150 Mbps)Much faster (up to ~1 Gbps on the right plan)
InterferenceCrowded — microwaves, Bluetooth, baby monitors, neighbours' Wi-FiCleaner, much less interference
Best forOlder & smart-home devices; rooms far from the routerStreaming, gaming, video calls; devices near the router
Use 2.4 GHz when…
  • the device is far from the router or through walls
  • it's an older phone/laptop or a smart-home gadget (plug, bulb, camera, doorbell)
  • the device only supports 2.4 GHz
Use 5 GHz when…
  • the device is close to the router
  • they want speed — streaming, gaming, video calls, big downloads
Keep both bands on. Most homes use both together — the goal isn't to pick one, it's to put each device on the band that suits it. (Newest gear may also have a 6 GHz / Wi-Fi 6E band: faster again, shortest range. Most customers only have 2.4 + 5 GHz.)

Band steering (some routers call it Smart Connect) puts both bands under one Wi-Fi name and lets the router decide which band each device uses.

With one name the router chooses the band — and often gets it wrong, so a device sticks to the slow band and drops. Splitting into two names lets you put each device on the right band.

Why it causes dropouts & slow speeds: in practice band steering is unreliable. A device often clings to the band it first joined, or bounces between bands, and smart-home/older devices crowd onto 2.4 GHz alongside phones and laptops that should be on 5 GHz. The result is drop-outs and slow speed over Wi-Fi.

The fix — split the bands In the router's wireless settings, turn off Band steering / Smart Connect. The router then shows two names; many add "-5G" to the 5 GHz name automatically. If not, give the 5 GHz network a name ending in "5G" or "5GHz" so it's easy to tell apart.

Then connect each device to the right name: close/fast devices to the …-5G network; older, smart-home and far-room devices to the normal (2.4 GHz) network. The password usually stays the same — devices just reconnect to the new name once.

Step 1 is quickest in the app — have the customer run an Origin Internet Helper scan / 5 GHz speed test next to the router to see whether Wi-Fi is the bottleneck, before changing any settings.

For "Wi-Fi keeps dropping" or "slow over Wi-Fi" calls, splitting the bands fixes the majority. Work it in this order:

1
Confirm it's Wi-Fi, not the line. Is it only over Wi-Fi? On a device plugged in by cable, is it fine? If the whole service is down, treat it as a connection fault (NBN-side T/S), not Wi-Fi.
2
Split the bands. In the router's wireless settings, turn off Band steering / Smart Connect (or add "-5G" to the 5 GHz name). Save — the router reboots briefly.
3
Put devices on the right band. Close/fast devices on the …-5G network; older, smart-home and far-room devices on the 2.4 GHz network. Reconnect them (re-enter the password if asked).
4
Quick wins if still poor. Move closer / clear obstacles; reduce 2.4 GHz interference (microwave, cordless phone, other gadgets); restart the router; check there aren't too many devices on one band.
5
Still dropping? Check the line / NBN side and the Wi-Fi channel, and escalate per the usual troubleshooting if it isn't a Wi-Fi issue.
The giveaway: Wi-Fi problems are inside the home and won't show as a line fault. A cabled device working fine while Wi-Fi struggles points straight at Wi-Fi — split the bands.

Plain-language lines you can use on the call:

The two lanes (analogy): "Think of your Wi-Fi as two lanes coming from the router. 2.4 GHz is a wide lane that reaches the whole house and even the yard, but traffic moves slower. 5 GHz is a fast lane — great speed — but it's shorter and doesn't pass through walls as well."
Why we're splitting it: "Right now all your devices share one Wi-Fi name and the router picks the lane for them — sometimes it picks the wrong one and you drop out. I'll split it into two names so each device can sit on the best lane."
Which device goes where: "Put your phone, laptop and TV near the router on the '…-5G' network for speed. Put older gadgets, smart bulbs or cameras, and anything in the far rooms on the normal network so they stay connected."
Reassure them: "Your password stays the same — you'll just tap the new network name once to reconnect."

Wi-Fi fades with distance, through every wall, and especially between floors. In this townhouse the router sits downstairs at the front, but one device is upstairs and another is in the back room — both far from it, so both start as dead spots. Drag the router to a better spot, or drop a mesh point upstairs to carry the signal. Try it:

Drag the router (and the mesh point) around the townhouse.
Upstairs device: Dead spot  ·  Back-room device: Dead spot
One router struggles to reach upstairs and the back room. Drag it more central, or tick "Add a mesh point" and drop it near the weak device.

What's a mesh? Instead of one router straining to cover the whole home, a mesh uses the main router plus one or more mesh points (nodes) placed around the house. They talk to each other and share one Wi-Fi name, so devices automatically hop to the nearest, strongest node as the customer moves around — no dead spots, no switching networks.

Better router placement first
  • central & out in the open — not in a cupboard or behind the TV
  • up high (a shelf), antennas upright
  • away from microwaves, cordless phones, fish tanks & thick walls
When to suggest a mesh
  • large or double-storey homes; thick / brick walls
  • dead spots in far rooms or outdoor areas
  • signal is fine near the router but poor elsewhere
Use Origin Internet Helper Don’t guess where the signal drops — get the customer to install Origin Internet Helper and run Find Dead Spots; it maps the weak areas room by room.
Origin Internet Helper

Our in-house tool for diagnosing Wi-Fi problems — slow speeds, drop-outs and dead spots. The customer runs it on their phone; you watch the results live.

Do this first Get the customer onto Origin Internet Helper and connected to their home Wi-Fi before any test. Two ways in: the App Store / Google Play app gives the full checks below; the web link is quicker but only does live video / photo (they’ll be asked to allow camera access). For speed tests and dead-spot scans, they need the app.
Slow speeds

In the app, get them to stand next to the router on 5 GHz, then run a quick speed test (Scan for new issues) and compare it against their plan speed.

Drop-outs & dead spots

Run Find Dead Spots (Live Range) and walk the customer around the home while it maps where the signal drops.

When the scan finishes it flags anything worth discussing across four areas — User devices, Wi-Fi network(s), Modem / router and Line quality. Work through whatever’s flagged with the customer.

1. Agent view — open the customer’s session and choose “Scan for new issues”.
Full guide
For the full Origin Internet Helper overview and step-by-step walkthrough, search Confluence for Origin Internet Helper - Telco Specialists.

Screens from Origin Internet Helper — tap an image to enlarge. For training only.

9 The isolation test

An isolation test takes Wi-Fi and the home network out of the picture: you plug one device straight into the NBN box with a network cable and run a speed test. A cable can’t have “bad reception,” so whatever it shows is the true speed coming into the home — the fastest way to tell whether to fix the Wi-Fi or escalate the line.

Plan 500 / 50 Mbps · two-storey home
strongokweak

Colour wash = live Wi-Fi coverage. Drag the router anywhere (try moving it upstairs), tap it to see its WAN / LAN ports, and drag the blue cable off the router’s WAN port onto a device to run an isolation test.

The NBN cable feeds the router's WAN port; every device is on Wi-Fi and below the plan - distance, walls and the upstairs floor all cost speed. Unplug the cable from the router and drop it on a device to run an isolation test.
Why it’s worth doingWi-Fi is almost never the full plan speed — walls, distance, an old phone and a busy channel all slow it down. So a slow result over Wi-Fi doesn’t prove anything is wrong with the connection. The wired test is the fair test, and it often saves the customer a technician visit when the line turns out to be fine.

How to run it

  1. Use a device with a network (RJ45 / Ethernet) port. A laptop or desktop PC is best — you can run a proper speed test in the browser. A PlayStation, Xbox or many smart TVs have one too (use their built-in connection / speed test).
  2. No Ethernet port? (a thin laptop, MacBook, phone or tablet) — grab a USB-A or USB-C to Ethernet adapter (match it to the device’s port) from an electronics / hardware store. For plans over 100 Mbps make sure it’s a Gigabit / USB 3.0 adapter, or it’ll cap the result.
  3. Cable straight to the NBN box. Run a Cat5e or Cat6 lead (both handle gigabit) from the NBN box’s data port (UNI-D / LAN) to the device — no router, no Wi-Fi in between.
  4. Run a speed test and compare it to the plan.
Reading the result · for specialistsWired ≈ plan speed → the line and plan are healthy; the bottleneck is the home network (Wi-Fi placement, bands, an old device) — fix the Wi-Fi. Wired still well below plan → the problem is on the line / NBN side, and that’s when an NBN fault / trouble ticket is justified. This is why we ask for it before escalating: when the NBN tests pass but the customer still reports slow speeds or drop-outs, the isolation test shows whether the fault is inside the home (which NBN won’t fix) or on the line.

Heads-up on plan speeds: the quoted plan speed is an “up to” line speed delivered to the NBN box — not a guarantee of speed over Wi-Fi, which depends on the customer’s own equipment and home layout. Confirm the exact plan-speed / responsibility wording against current Origin policy before quoting it to a customer.

All of the above is simplified for training and uses example values. Exact pool ranges, RADIUS attributes and CGNAT policy vary by network and product — always follow your team's current process and tooling for live customer cases.

NBN FAULT
If troubleshooting fails, or the issue is on the NBN side — go to Confluence and search Lodging a Trouble Ticket to NBN, or escalate to L2 as per Confluence. Always follow Confluence for the correct steps.
FTTB — Fibre to the Building (shares this tab)
FTTB is for apartments and units. NBN runs fibre to the building's comms room or basement — to the nbn DSLAM and nbn termination modules — and from there the building's existing copper carries the service through the building's MDF (main distribution frame) and the floor IDFs to each unit. The in-unit setup is the same as FTTN: a VDSL2 modem, DSL sync, then Dynamic IP — so for the modem side, use the FTTN simulator and steps above.
Key difference For FTTB (and FTTN at an apartment / MDU site) the network boundary is the building MDF — not the wall socket inside the unit. The copper beyond the MDF (MDF → the unit) is the customer's / body corporate's responsibility to maintain and fix, not NBN's.
NBN brings the service in from the street to the building MDF — the network boundary. Everything past the MDF (the riser copper, the floor IDFs and the cabling into each unit's TO) sits on the customer side.
How "Open Circuit beyond MDF" appears in the SHS (ANEX)
Run the Service Health check in ANEX. The giveaways: nbn Technology Type — FTTB, Service Status — Not Eligible: Service Offline, Open Circuit past MDF, and the Summary naming an open circuit beyond the MDF; Connectivity shows a cross. (Account / ID values are placeholders.)
Open Circuit beyond MDF If the SHS in ANEX shows the service offline with "Open Circuit beyond MDF" (service not eligible / offline, open circuit past the MDF), the break is in the copper on the customer side of the boundary — between the unit and the building MDF. Don't raise a standard service-restoration ticket — it will almost certainly come back No Fault Found. Instead:
1
Explain it's the customer's / body corporate's responsibility (the fault is beyond the NBN boundary) — the customer needs to engage their own (private) cabler to investigate the copper between the unit and the MDF.
2
Raise a Trouble Ticket to L2 requesting the MDF pair information for the service.
3
Tell the customer to call us back for the pair information once their cabler is booked in, so the cabler has what they need to trace and fix the correct pair at the MDF.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
FTTN — Fibre to the Node · how it connects
Fibre runs to a street cabinet (the node); the last stretch to the home is the existing copper phone line, which the modem syncs over. Press Run to watch it connect, or Step through one stage at a time.
Node
DSLAM
Wall socket
NBN / Telstra
Modem
DSL port at back
Internet
the web
Ready
Press Run to watch FTTN connect, or Step one stage at a time.
Item detail
Power
off
DSL
off
Internet
offline
Troubleshooting steps
Always work through these in order ↓
1
Find the DSL wall socket. Use the first phone socket at the property — usually labelled NBN or Telstra.
↓
2
Plug the modem in. Connect it to that wall socket with the DSL / phone cable — into the port labelled DSL (not a yellow LAN port).
↓
3
Wait for the DSL light. It flashes while connecting, then goes solid green — that means the line is in sync.
↓
4
Check the Internet light. If there's no Internet light, log in to the modem and set the connection to Dynamic IP.
↓
5
Confirm it's online. Internet light green, and the auth / session log shows the connection.
↓
6
Test it. Open a website to make sure it's actually working.
Follow steps 1 → 6 in order. The simulation highlights where you are.
Subscriber session · proof the line is onlineNo data
Example only — the same back-office view as the DHCP tab. Set the modem to Dynamic IP and Save, and the session fills in (note the DHCP timestamp) proving the line is online.
AVC/OAC ID: —
Framed IP: —
MAC: —
Success (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ACCT (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
DHCP (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
Quick troubleshooting & escalation
Before you start: confirm there's no network outage and no account / provisioning issue, and that SHS and the initial checks are done. Use the Origin Internet Helper · Telco Specialists where it applies.
1 · Initial check & power-cycle→2 · Check the DSL line→3 · Dynamic IP→4 · Factory reset→5 · Escalate (L2)
1 · Read the modem lights — each one tells you where to go next
Light
—
What it means → next step
Power
Power
Green solid = on and operating normally → check the DSL light. Red solid = modem fault or power-supply issue → try the supplied power adapter; still red → Step 5. Off = no power: check it's plugged in and switched on.
DSL
Line
Green solid = synced to the FTTN network → check Internet. Green flashing = trying to sync (give it a few minutes). Red (solid or flashing) or off = can't connect to the FTTN network → go to Step 2 (check the cabling / line); won't sync → Step 5.
Internet
Line
Green solid = connected to the internet. Green flashing = data. Red solid = no internet: if DSL is solid green, go to Step 3 (set Dynamic IP).
Wi-Fi
Wi-Fi
Green solid = Wi-Fi on and broadcasting. Green flashing = data. Red / off = Wi-Fi turned off — turn it back on (a Wi-Fi-only issue, not a line fault).
2 · Work the steps in order — follow where each light sends you
1
Initial check & power-cycle. Confirm the modem is powered, and the phone/DSL lead runs from the modem's DSL port (not a yellow LAN port) to the first wall socket with a good cable. Remove any splitters / filters, extension leads and extra devices on the line (a phone needs a microfilter). Power-cycle the modem (off ~20–30s) and re-read the lights. If it's a BYO modem, confirm it's VDSL2 / FTTN-compatible.
2
Check the DSL line (when the DSL light is red, off, or won't go solid). Swap the phone/DSL lead, plug straight into the first / main wall socket, and remove any splitters / filters; then re-test via SHS. The DSL light should go solid green once it syncs. → Syncs → go to Step 3. → Won't sync → likely a copper line fault → Step 5.
3
Check it's online (DSL solid green but no internet). In the modem, confirm the connection is set to Dynamic IP (IPoE / obtain automatically — NBN FTTN doesn't use a PPPoE username & password), then reboot or reprovision. → Online → confirm with the customer. → Still no internet → go to Step 4.
4
Mandatory script — read before reset Advise the customer: “Please be aware that performing a factory reset on your modem/router will erase all customised settings. This includes but is not limited to WiFi details such as SSIDs, passwords, security settings, and channels, VoIP provisioning details for BYO devices, and any custom port forwarding settings. Do you understand and agree to proceed with the factory reset?”
Factory-reset the modem/router — get consent first (wipes Wi-Fi name / password & custom settings). Reconfigure it as a VDSL2 connection with Dynamic IP; if possible, test an alternate VDSL2-compatible modem to rule out a faulty unit. TP-Link Archer VR1600v / VR400 → set Operation Mode to “Wireless Router mode”. → Still no connectivity → Step 5 (points to the modem or the line). → Resolved → confirm.
5
Escalate to Level 2. Raise a Trouble Ticket (correct fault type — typically a copper line / node fault), document every step and customer interaction in ANEX, and advise the customer of the escalation.
No fault found? VPN on → ask them to turn it off. Splitters / filters or damaged cables → remove or replace. Wi-Fi only → check Wi-Fi settings. Pages won't load → clear the browser cache / check DNS. Faulty or incompatible modem → offer an Origin-supplied device.
Escalation Lodge the NBN fault through Confluence — search Lodging a Trouble Ticket to NBN — or escalate to L2 as per Confluence. A tech appointment is reserved by default for L2 fault escalations. If one is needed, capture three customer availability windows at least 2 business days out, and advise the customer that someone 18+ must be home and that the window isn't guaranteed. If no appointment is needed, document the justification in the ticket.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
FTTC — Fibre to the Curb · how it connects
Fibre runs to a small box (the DPU) in a pit out on the street; the existing copper covers the short last stretch to the property. Inside the home, an NBN connection box (NCD) plugs into the first wall socket — it powers the street DPU and gives the router an Ethernet port to plug into. Press Run to watch it connect, or Step through it.
DPU
street pit
Wall socket
NBN / Telstra
NBN box
NCD
Router
WAN → gateway
Internet
the web
Ready
Press Run to watch FTTC connect, or Step one stage at a time.
NBN box (NCD) close-up
① Phone / DSL cable: wall socket → NCD DSL port  ·  ② Ethernet: NCD Gateway → router WAN. Always use the router's WAN / Internet port — never a LAN port.
NCD · Power
off
NCD · Link
off
NCD · DSL
off
NCD · LAN
off
Router · WAN
off
Router · Internet
off
Blue solid = good  ·  Blue blinking = updating, wait up to 15 min  ·  Link red = NBN-side problem → escalate  ·  LAN amber = connected but only ~100 Mbps  ·  Off = not connected
Router · WAN = green once the Ethernet link is up  ·  Router · Internet = stays amber (no IP) until you set Dynamic IP, then turns green.
Troubleshooting steps
Always work through these in order ↓
1
Power & connect the NBN box. Plug the NBN box (NCD) into power and into the first wall socket (NBN / Telstra) with the grey cable. It powers the street box, so it must stay on.
↓
2
Wait for the lights. They flash while connecting, then Link and DSL go solid blue — the NBN box is talking to the network.
↓
3
Connect the router. Once both are solid blue, run an Ethernet cable from the NBN box's gateway port to the router's WAN port.
↓
4
Check the Internet light. If there's no Internet light, log in to the router and set the connection to Dynamic IP.
↓
5
Confirm it's online. The LAN light is active and the auth / session log shows the connection.
↓
6
Test it. Open a website to make sure it's working.
Follow steps 1 → 6 in order. The simulation highlights where you are.
Subscriber session · proof the line is onlineNo data
Example only — the same back-office view as the DHCP tab. Set the router to Dynamic IP and Save, and the session fills in (note the DHCP timestamp), proving the line is online.
AVC / OAC ID: —
Framed IP: —
MAC: —
Success (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ACCT (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
DHCP (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
Quick troubleshooting & escalation
Before you start: confirm there's no network outage and no account / provisioning issue, and that SHS and the initial checks are done. Use the Origin Internet Helper · Telco Specialists where it applies.
1 · Initial check→2 · Ethernet→3 · Phone cabling→4 · Factory reset→5 · Escalate (L2)
1 · Read the NCD lights — each one tells you where to go next
Light
—
What it means → next step
Power
Power
Blue = powered → check the Link light. Off = no power: check for an outage and that the box is on a known-good outlet. Still off → Step 5.
Link
Link
Blue solid = good → check DSL. Blue blinking = updating, wait up to ~15 min. Red / Off = NBN-side problem → Step 3.
DSL
DSL
Blue solid = talking to the NBN → check LAN. Blue blinking = syncing, wait up to ~15 min. Off = broadband link down → Step 3.
LAN
LAN
Blue = router linked (blinking = data flowing). Amber = linked but only ~100 Mbps. Off = no Ethernet to the router → Step 2.
2 · Work the steps in order — follow where each light sends you
1
Initial check & power-cycle. Confirm the NBN box is powered and plugged into the first wall socket via the DSL port with a good phone cable; remove any splitters / filters and extra devices; check the Ethernet from the router WAN to the gateway port; then power-cycle both (off 20–30s) and re-read the lights. BYO modem → confirm it's FTTC-compatible.
2
Test the Ethernet / another device (when LAN is off). Re-plug the Ethernet, try another cable, and bypass the router by plugging another device straight into the gateway port. → LAN still off = likely NBN-box (NCD) hardware fault → Step 5. → LAN comes up but the original router was at fault → Step 4.
3
Test the phone cabling (when Link / DSL is red or off). Swap the phone cable between the wall socket and the NBN box's DSL port, then re-test via SHS. → Working → confirm with the customer. → Can't test, or still red/off → Step 5.
4
Mandatory script — read before reset Advise the customer: “Please be aware that performing a factory reset on your modem/router will erase all customised settings. This includes but is not limited to WiFi details such as SSIDs, passwords, security settings, and channels, VoIP provisioning details for BYO devices, and any custom port forwarding settings. Do you understand and agree to proceed with the factory reset?”
Factory-reset the router — get consent first (wipes Wi-Fi name / password & custom settings). Reconnect with an alternate Ethernet if possible, hold the reset button ~10s (paperclip), then reconfigure. TP-Link Archer VR1600v / VR400 → set Operation Mode to “Wireless Router mode”. → Still no connectivity → Step 5 (points to the router; possible replacement). → Resolved → confirm.
5
Escalate to Level 2. Raise a Trouble Ticket (correct fault type), document every step and customer interaction in ANEX, and advise the customer of the escalation.
No fault found? VPN on → ask them to turn it off. Splitters / filters or damaged cables → remove or replace. Wi-Fi only → check Wi-Fi settings. Pages won't load → clear the browser cache / check DNS. Faulty or incompatible modem → offer an Origin-supplied device.
Escalation Lodge the NBN fault through Confluence — search Lodging a Trouble Ticket to NBN — or escalate to L2 as per Confluence. A tech appointment is reserved by default for L2 fault escalations. If one is needed, capture three customer availability windows at least 2 business days out, and advise the customer that someone 18+ must be home and that the window isn't guaranteed. If no appointment is needed, document the justification in the ticket.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
HFC — Hybrid Fibre-Coaxial · how it connects
On HFC, the NBN signal arrives over the existing coaxial (pay-TV / cable) line to a coax wall outlet. An NBN connection box (NTD) plugs into that outlet and gives the router an Ethernet port — the UNI-D1 port — to plug into. Press Run to watch it connect, or Step through it.
HFC network
street tap
Coax outlet
wall
NBN box
NTD
Router
WAN → UNI-D1
Internet
the web
Ready
Press Run to watch HFC connect, or Step one stage at a time.
NBN box (NTD) close-up
① Coax cable: wall outlet → NTD coax port  ·  ② Ethernet: NTD UNI-D1 → router WAN. Always use the router's WAN / Internet port — never a LAN port.
NTD · Power
off
NTD · Downstream
off
NTD · Upstream
off
NTD · Online
off
NTD · UNI-D1
off
Router · WAN
off
Router · Internet
off
Solid green = good  ·  Flashing = connecting / updating, wait 5–10 min  ·  Online off = no NBN connection → escalate  ·  UNI-D1 green = 1 Gbps · amber = 100 Mbps  ·  Off = not connected
Router · WAN = green once the Ethernet link is up  ·  Router · Internet = stays amber (no IP) until you set Dynamic IP, then turns green.
Troubleshooting steps
Always work through these in order ↓
1
Power & connect the NTD. Plug the NBN box (NTD) into power and into the coax wall outlet with the coaxial cable, finger-tight at both ends. Remove any unnecessary splitters.
↓
2
Wait for the lights. Downstream and Upstream go solid, then Online comes on — the NTD is talking to the NBN. This can take 5–10 minutes.
↓
3
Connect the router. Once Online is on, run an Ethernet cable from the router's WAN port to the NTD's UNI-D1 port.
↓
4
Check it's online. If there's no internet, log in to the router and set the connection to Dynamic IP.
↓
5
Confirm it's online. The UNI-D1 link light is on and the auth / session log shows the connection.
↓
6
Test it. Open a website to make sure it's working.
Follow steps 1 → 6 in order. The simulation highlights where you are.
Subscriber session · proof the line is onlineNo data
Example only — the same back-office view as the DHCP tab. Set the router to Dynamic IP and Save, and the session fills in (note the DHCP timestamp), proving the line is online.
AVC / OAC ID: —
Framed IP: —
MAC: —
Success (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ACCT (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
DHCP (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
Quick troubleshooting & escalation
Before you start: confirm there's no network outage and no account / provisioning issue, and that SHS and the initial checks are done. Use the Origin Internet Helper · Telco Specialists where it applies.
1 · Initial check→2 · UNI-D1 test→3 · Cable / device→4 · Factory reset→5 · Escalate (L2)
1 · Read the NTD lights — each one tells you where to go next
Light
—
What it means → next step
Power
Power
On = powered → check Downstream / Upstream. Off = no power: test another working power outlet. Still off → Step 5.
Downstream + Upstream
Line
Flashing + Online off = no NBN connection → Step 5. Flashing + Online on = firmware update — wait 5–10 min, re-power-cycle; still flashing → Step 5.
Online
Line
Flashing (Down/Up solid) = retrieving setup from the NBN — wait 5–10 min; if it stays flashing → Step 5. Solid / on = connected to the NBN → go to Step 2.
UNI-D1
Port
Off = no cable detected → Step 3. Green = 1 Gbps link (confirm service; Step 3 if still no internet). Yellow / amber = 10/100 Mbps — try a better Ethernet cable.
2 · Work the steps in order — follow where each light sends you
1
Initial check & power-cycle. Confirm the NTD is powered and connected to the coax wall outlet with the coax cable tight at both ends; remove any unnecessary splitters; then power-cycle the NTD and router (off ~30s) and re-read the lights above.
2
Isolation test to the UNI-D1 port (once Online is on). Make sure the Ethernet runs from the router's WAN to the NTD's UNI-D1 port, and check the UNI-D1 link light. → Off → Step 3. → Green / amber but still no internet → Step 3.
3
Test the cable / another device. Re-plug the Ethernet, try another cable, and bypass the router by plugging another device straight into UNI-D1. → Works on a new cable = faulty cable → confirm. → Works on another device = router at fault → Step 4. → Can't test → document → Step 4. → Still nothing → document → Step 5.
4
Mandatory script — read before reset Advise the customer: “Please be aware that performing a factory reset on your modem/router will erase all customised settings. This includes but is not limited to WiFi details such as SSIDs, passwords, security settings, and channels, VoIP provisioning details for BYO devices, and any custom port forwarding settings. Do you understand and agree to proceed with the factory reset?”
Factory-reset the router — get consent first (wipes Wi-Fi name / password & custom settings). Reconnect with an alternate Ethernet if possible, hold the reset button ~10s (paperclip), then reconfigure. TP-Link Archer VR1600v / VR400 → set Operation Mode to “Wireless Router mode”. → Still no connectivity → Step 5 (points to the router; possible replacement). → Resolved → confirm.
5
Escalate to Level 2. Raise a Trouble Ticket (correct fault type), document every step and customer interaction in ANEX, and advise the customer of the escalation.
No fault found? VPN on → ask them to turn it off. Splitters or damaged cables → remove or replace. Wi-Fi only → check Wi-Fi settings. Pages won't load → clear the browser cache / check DNS. Faulty or incompatible modem → offer an Origin-supplied device.
Escalation Lodge the NBN fault through Confluence — search Lodging a Trouble Ticket to NBN — or escalate to L2 as per Confluence. A tech appointment is reserved by default for L2 fault escalations. If one is needed, capture three customer availability windows at least 2 business days out, and advise the customer that someone 18+ must be home and that the window isn't guaranteed. If no appointment is needed, document the justification in the ticket.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
FTTP — Fibre to the Premises · how it connects
On FTTP, a fibre-optic cable runs all the way to the premises — into the PCD (utility box) outside, then to the NBN connection box (NTD) inside. The NTD turns the fibre light into Ethernet, giving the router a UNI-D port to plug into. Press Run to watch it connect, or Step through it.
Fibre network
exchange
PCD
utility box
NBN box
NTD
Router
WAN → UNI-D
Internet
the web
Ready
Press Run to watch FTTP connect, or Step one stage at a time.
NBN box (NTD) close-up
① Fibre (green): PCD → NTD Optical port — never unplug the fibre. ② Ethernet: router WAN → the active UNI-D port (usually UNI-D1; check SHS — it can move to UNI-D2–4 after a provider switch). UNI-V = phone, not internet.
NTD · Power
off
NTD · Optical
off
UNI-D1 · active
off
UNI-D2
off
UNI-D3
off
UNI-D4
off
Router · WAN
off
Router · Internet
off
Power: green = ready (red = on battery, off = no power)  ·  Optical: green = connected to fibre, red = lost fibre  ·  UNI-D (the active port only): orange = 1 Gbps, green = 10/100 Mbps, off = nothing plugged in. Only one UNI-D is active — check SHS (Port Id), usually UNI-D1.
Router · WAN = green once the Ethernet link is up  ·  Router · Internet = stays amber (no IP) until you set Dynamic IP, then turns green.
Troubleshooting steps
Always work through these in order ↓
1
Power & connect the NTD. Make sure the NTD is powered and the green fibre (Optical) lead is in — don't unplug the fibre unless told to.
↓
2
Wait for the lights. Power goes solid green, then Optical goes solid green — the NTD is connected to the fibre network. Alarm should be off.
↓
3
Connect the router. Run an Ethernet cable from the router's WAN port to the active UNI-D port on the NTD's underside — usually UNI-D1, but confirm on SHS (Port Id). Not a UNI-V (phone) port.
↓
4
Check it's online. If there's no internet, log in to the router and set the connection to Dynamic IP.
↓
5
Confirm it's online. The UNI-D light is on and the auth / session log shows the connection.
↓
6
Test it. Open a website to make sure it's working.
Follow steps 1 → 6 in order. The simulation highlights where you are.
Subscriber session · proof the line is onlineNo data
Example only — the same back-office view as the DHCP tab. Set the router to Dynamic IP and Save, and the session fills in (note the DHCP timestamp), proving the line is online.
AVC / OAC ID: —
Framed IP: —
MAC: —
Success (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ACCT (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
DHCP (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
Quick troubleshooting & escalation
Before you start: confirm there's no network outage and no account / provisioning issue, and that SHS and the initial checks are done. Use the Origin Internet Helper · Telco Specialists where it applies. Some NTDs need the cover removed to see the lights.
1 · NTD lights→2 · Power-cycle NTD→3 · UNI-D test→4 · Factory reset→5 · Serial number→6 · Escalate (L2)
Use the active UNI-D port An FTTP NTD has up to four UNI-D ports (UNI-D 1–4), but only one is provisioned for the service. It's usually UNI-D1, but it can be UNI-D2–4 (e.g. after a provider switch). Confirm the active port on SHS → General Information (Port Id) and plug the router's WAN into that UNI-D port. The wrong UNI-D, or a UNI-V (phone) port, gives no internet. A single-port NTD only has UNI-D1 — use that.
1 · Read the NTD lights — each one tells you where to go next
Light
—
What it means → next step
Power
Power
Green = has power (may look orange/yellow — that's fine) → check Optical. Red = running on backup battery (PSU). Off = no power: ensure no outage, PSU plugged in / switched on / connected, router on a known-good outlet; still off → Step 6.
Optical
Fibre
Green = connected to the fibre network (flashes on data) → check UNI-D. Red = optical link disconnected. Off = NTD disabled. Red / off → Step 2. If Optical is green but still no service: SHS Connectivity red → Step 6; green → Step 3.
UNI-D 1–4
Ports
Read the active port (per SHS, usually UNI-D1). Orange = router linked at 1 Gbps (flashes on data). Green = linked at 10/100 Mbps. Off = nothing plugged into that port → Step 3. The other UNI-D ports stay off unless a separate service is provisioned.
2 · Work the steps in order — follow where each light sends you
1
Evaluate the NTD lights. Read Power → Optical → UNI-D as above and follow where they point. (Some NTDs need the cover popped off to see the lights — unclip the bottom.)
2
Power-cycle the NTD. Turn it off, wait ~10 seconds, turn it back on; re-test in SHS and re-check the Optical light. → Optical green → confirm and re-check the lights. → Optical off / red → Step 6.
3
Isolation test to the UNI-D port. First confirm the active UNI-D port on SHS → General Information (Port Id) — usually UNI-D1, but may be UNI-D2–4. Make sure the router's WAN is in that port (underside of the NTD), not a UNI-V (phone) port. Power-cycle the router (off 20–30s), then try another Ethernet cable and another device straight into the active UNI-D port. → New cable works = faulty cable. → Another device works = router at fault → Step 4. → Can't test → document → Step 4. → Still nothing → document → Step 6.
4
Mandatory script — read before reset Advise the customer: “Please be aware that performing a factory reset on your modem/router will erase all customised settings. This includes but is not limited to WiFi details such as SSIDs, passwords, security settings, and channels, VoIP provisioning details for BYO devices, and any custom port forwarding settings. Do you understand and agree to proceed with the factory reset?”
Factory-reset the router — get consent first (wipes Wi-Fi name / password & custom settings). Reconnect with an alternate Ethernet if possible, hold the reset button ~10s (paperclip), then reconfigure. TP-Link Archer VR1600v / VR400 → set Operation Mode to “Wireless Router mode”. → Still no connectivity → Step 6. → Resolved → confirm.
5
Confirm the NTD serial number. Find it on the barcode by the UNI-D ports (starts “ALCL”) or by scanning the QR on the front of the NTD, and compare with SHS / ANEX. → Matches but still down → Step 6. → Doesn't match → reconfirm the service address (possible provisioning / jumpering issue; may need a photo via RouteThis or a New Sale + Proof of Occupancy). → Can't obtain → document → Step 6.
6
Escalate to Level 2. Raise a Trouble Ticket (correct fault type), document every step and customer interaction in ANEX, and advise the customer of the escalation.
No fault found? VPN on → ask them to turn it off. Splitters / filters or damaged cables → remove or replace. Wi-Fi only → check Wi-Fi settings. Pages won't load → clear the browser cache / check DNS. Faulty or incompatible modem → offer an Origin-supplied device.
Battery backup: some FTTP installs have a battery backup on the power supply (keeps phone working in an outage). Origin can't replace the NTD battery — the customer arranges it themselves. The standard cell (PSUs installed before June 2024) is a 12 V 7.0–7.2 Ah 6-cell SLA battery (F2 terminals), available from specialist battery retailers.
Escalation Lodge the NBN fault through Confluence — search Lodging a Trouble Ticket to NBN — or escalate to L2 as per Confluence. A tech appointment is reserved by default for L2 fault escalations. If one is needed, capture three customer availability windows at least 2 business days out, and advise the customer that someone 18+ must be home and that the window isn't guaranteed. If no appointment is needed, document the justification in the ticket.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
FW — Fixed Wireless · how it connects
Fixed Wireless is used where running cable isn't practical — mostly regional and remote areas. The NBN signal travels by radio from a nearby NBN tower to a small outdoor antenna on the roof, which feeds an indoor NBN connection box (NTD). The router plugs into the NTD's UNI-D port. Press Run to watch it connect, or Step through it.
NBN tower
radio
Outdoor antenna
on the roof
NBN box
NTD
Router
WAN → UNI-D1
Internet
the web
Ready
Press Run to watch Fixed Wireless connect, or Step one stage at a time.
NBN box (NTD) close-up
① Antenna cable: roof antenna → NTD antenna port — never unplug it  ·  ② Ethernet: NTD UNI-D1 → router WAN. Always use the router's WAN / Internet port — never a LAN port.
NTD · Power
off
NTD · Status
off
NTD · ODU
off
NTD · Signal
off
Router · WAN
off
Router · Internet
off
Power green = on (flashing = on battery)  ·  Status green = talking to the NBN (amber flash = starting up, wait 5–10 min; red = fault)  ·  ODU green = link to the tower is up (red = link down → escalate)  ·  Signal green = strong · amber = medium · red = weak but no fault.
Router · WAN = green once the Ethernet link to UNI-D is up  ·  Router · Internet = stays amber (no IP) until you set Dynamic IP, then turns green.
Troubleshooting steps
Always work through these in order ↓
1
Power & connect the NTD. Plug the NBN box (NTD) into power, and make sure the antenna cable from the roof is firmly connected to the NTD's antenna port. Never unplug the antenna cable.
↓
2
Wait for the lights. Status comes up, then ODU goes green (the link to the tower), and Signal shows strength. This can take 5–10 minutes.
↓
3
Connect the router. Run an Ethernet cable from the router's WAN port to the NTD's provisioned UNI-D port (usually UNI-D1).
↓
4
Check it's online. If there's no internet, log in to the router and set the connection to Dynamic IP.
↓
5
Confirm it's online. The router's WAN link light is on and the auth / session log shows the connection.
↓
6
Test it. Open a website to make sure it's working.
Follow steps 1 → 6 in order. The simulation highlights where you are.
Subscriber session · proof the line is onlineNo data
Example only — the same back-office view as the DHCP tab. Set the router to Dynamic IP and Save, and the session fills in (note the DHCP timestamp), proving the line is online.
AVC / OAC ID: —
Framed IP: —
MAC: —
Success (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ACCT (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
DHCP (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
Quick troubleshooting & escalation
Before you start: confirm there's no network outage and no account / provisioning issue, and that SHS and the initial checks are done. Use the Origin Internet Helper · Telco Specialists where it applies.
Symptoms / common causes: no internet on any device · Power light off · Status red · ODU red → a faulty NTD, a faulty modem/router, an external network issue, or an internal power problem / outage.
1 · Initial check & power-cycle→2 · UNI-D test→3 · Factory reset→4 · Escalate (L2)
1 · Read the NTD lights — each one tells you where to go next
Light
—
What it means → next step
Power
Power
Green solid = powered → check Status. Green flashing = on backup battery: check for a power outage, confirm the modem is on a working outlet; if power stays off → Step 4. Red / off = no power: try another working outlet; still off → Step 4.
Status
Line
Green (solid or flashing) = talking to the NBN → check ODU. Amber flashing = starting up: allow 5–10 min, re-power-cycle; still persisting → Step 4. Red solid = system fault → Step 4.
ODU
Line
Green (solid or flashing) = the link out to the tower is up → go to Step 2. Red solid = broadband link down. Red flashing = connection error → Step 4.
Signal
Signal
Green = high · Amber = medium · Red = low signal — this on its own is not a fault. If every other light is OK → go to Step 2.
2 · Work the steps in order — follow where each light sends you
1
Initial check & power-cycle. Confirm the NTD is on a known-good power outlet; that every cable between the NTD and the antenna is intact and secure; that the antenna cable is attached with no obvious damage; and that an Ethernet cable runs from the router's WAN to the provisioned UNI-D port (usually UNI-D1). Power-cycle the NTD and router (off ~30s) and re-read the lights above. Status red solid or ODU red → Step 4.
2
Isolation test to the UNI-D port. Confirm the router is powered and connected to the assigned UNI-D port via its WAN port. Still not connecting → test an alternate Ethernet cable, then an alternate device (another router or a PC/laptop) into the assigned UNI-D port. → Works on a new cable = faulty cable → confirm. → Works on another device = router at fault → Step 3. → Can't test → document → Step 3. → Still nothing → document → Step 4.
3
Mandatory script — read before reset Advise the customer: “Please be aware that performing a factory reset on your modem/router will erase all customised settings. This includes but is not limited to WiFi details such as SSIDs, passwords, security settings, and channels, VoIP provisioning details for BYO devices, and any custom port forwarding settings. Do you understand and agree to proceed with the factory reset?”
Factory-reset the router — get consent first (wipes Wi-Fi name / password / security / channels, VoIP provisioning on BYO devices, and custom port forwarding). Reconnect with an alternate Ethernet cable if possible, hold the reset button ~10s (paperclip), then reconfigure. → Still no connectivity → Step 4 (points to the router; possible replacement). → Resolved → confirm.
4
Escalate to Level 2. Raise a Trouble Ticket (correct fault type), document every step and customer interaction, advise the customer, and leave a note in ANEX.
No fault found? VPN on → ask them to turn it off. Splitters or damaged cables → remove or replace, or have internal wiring checked. Wi-Fi only → check Wi-Fi settings and environment. Pages won't load → clear the browser cache / check DNS. Faulty or incompatible modem → offer an Origin-supplied device.
Escalation Lodge the NBN fault through Confluence — search Lodging a Trouble Ticket to NBN — or escalate to L2 as per Confluence. A tech appointment is reserved by default for L2 fault escalations. If one is needed, capture three customer availability windows at least 2 business days out, and advise the customer that someone 18+ must be home and that the window isn't guaranteed. If no appointment is needed, document the justification in the ticket.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
Fixed Wireless speeds & coverage — what to tell customers
Plain-English background for speed conversations. These are NBN wholesale capabilities — the speed a customer actually sees depends on their plan, the tower, distance, line-of-sight and busy-hour congestion. Always go by the customer's real plan and your team's current process.
Wholesale tiers (after the Fixed Wireless upgrade):
• FW Plus — up to 100/20 Mbps. The common plan; lifted from up to 75/10, now available across 100% of the Fixed Wireless footprint.
• FW Home Fast — up to 200–250 / 8–20 Mbps. Higher-speed tier at select locations (about 90% of upgraded areas).
• FW Superfast — up to 400 / 10–40 Mbps. Top tier at select locations (about 80%); only some retailers offer it.
What's "guaranteed"? There's no hard per-customer guarantee on Fixed Wireless, but the upgraded network is built to deliver a typical busy-period wholesale download of at least 50 Mbps in upgraded areas (busy hours are 7–11pm). Because it's radio, real speed varies with distance from the tower, trees and metal roofs / sheds — so many retailers don't make evening speed guarantees on Fixed Wireless.
Heads up The faster tiers (Home Fast / Superfast) need a newer NTD (WNTD v3 / v4) — NBN upgrades the device automatically when one of those plans is ordered if the current unit can't support it. Don't promise a tier before the address and hardware are confirmed. And note: the 25/5 Mbps "raise it if it's not met once a day" rule is for Sky Muster satellite — not Fixed Wireless.
Speed figures are NBN wholesale capabilities current at build time, for training context — verify against the live plan and current NBN / retail information before quoting a customer.
OptiComm — Fibre to the Premises (non-NBN) · how it connects
OptiComm is a separate fibre network (not NBN) used in many newer estates and developments. Fibre runs from the OptiComm network to a Premises Connection Device (PCD) at the property, then to the Optical Network Terminal (ONT) — the fibre box. The router plugs into the ONT's activated LAN port (only one LAN port is active) and is set to Dynamic IP (IPoE). Press Run to watch it connect, or Step through it.
OptiComm fibre
the network
PCD
premises box
ONT
fibre box
Router
WAN → LAN port
Internet
the web
Ready
Press Run to watch an OptiComm service connect, or Step one stage at a time.
Subscriber session · proof the line is onlineNo data
Example only — the same back-office view as the DHCP tab. Set the router to Dynamic IP (IPoE) and Save, and the session fills in (note the DHCP timestamp), proving the line is online.
AVC / OAC ID: —
Framed IP: —
MAC: —
Success (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ACCT (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
DHCP (Timestamp: N/A)
No data.
ONT close-up
① Fibre: OptiComm fibre → ONT optical port — never unplug it  ·  ② Ethernet: ONT activated LAN port (underside) → router WAN. Only one LAN port is activated — confirm it in Anex → Service tab (UNID Port); for migrated customers it may be wrong, so try all 4.
ONT · Power
off
ONT · PON/LOS
off
ONT · ALM
off · normal
ONT · Internet
off
ONT · LAN
off
Power green = on (off = no power → Step 4)  ·  PON/LOS green = optic signal normal, registered (ONCI) · red = no signal / not registered → escalate (Step 6) · flashing = firmware downloading (wait 5–10 min) · off → Step 4  ·  ALM off = normal · red = fault → Step 4.
Internet green = in service (off = not in service → Step 4)  ·  LAN green = link to the router is up (off = link down → Step 2). It stays amber (no IP) until you set Dynamic IP, then turns green.
Troubleshooting steps
Always work through these in order ↓
1
Power & connect the ONT. Plug the ONT into a known-good power outlet (OptiComm uses no battery — direct power). The ONT may be inside, outside, or in a building's common comms room. Connect an Ethernet cable from the router's WAN to the ONT's activated LAN port.
↓
2
Wait for the lights. Power goes solid, then PON/LOS goes green (ranged, authenticated and registered — ONCI success); ALM stays off. This can take a few minutes (longer if firmware is downloading).
↓
3
Connect the router. Run Ethernet from the router's WAN to the ONT's activated LAN port (underside). Only one LAN port is active — confirm it in Anex → Service tab (UNID Port).
↓
4
Check it's online. If there's no internet, log in to the router and set the connection to Dynamic IP (IPoE).
↓
5
Confirm it's online. The ONT's Internet light is green (in service) and the auth / session log shows the connection.
↓
6
Test it. Open a website to make sure it's working.
Follow steps 1 → 6 in order. The simulation highlights where you are.
Quick troubleshooting & escalation
Before you start: confirm there's no network outage and no account / provisioning issue, and that the initial checks are done. Use the Origin Internet Helper · Telco Specialists where it applies.
Symptoms / common causes: no internet on any device · Power light off · PON/LOS red or off · ALM red · LAN off → an external network issue, an internal power problem / outage, faulty internal equipment, a configuration / provisioning issue, an installation issue, or an externally-hosted issue (e.g. DNS).
1 · ONT lights & power-cycle→2 · CPE & cabling→3 · Isolation test→4 · Factory reset (IPoE)→5 · PPPoE via ZTE ACS→6 · Escalate (L2)
1 · Read the ONT lights — each one tells you where to go next
Light
—
What it means → next step
Power
Power
Green = the ONT is on → check PON/LOS. Off = the ONT is off / no power → check the outlet and restart it (Step 4).
PON/LOS
Line
Green = optic signal normal, registered (ONCI success) → check ALM. Red = no optic signal, not registered → Step 6. Blinking = firmware downloading → wait 5–10 min, else Step 4. Off = not connected to the fibre / no optical signal → Step 4.
ALM
Line
Off = optical power normal → check Internet. Red = no optical signal, firmware-update fail or other fault → Step 4.
Internet
Line
Green = in service → check LAN. Off = not in service → Step 4.
LAN 1–4
Link
Green = the link to the router is up → Step 3. Blinking = sending / receiving data → Step 3. Off = the link is down → Step 2.
2 · Work the steps in order — follow where each light sends you
1
Evaluate the ONT lights & power-cycle. Confirm the ONT is powered from a known-good outlet (power cable secure) and that an Ethernet cable runs from the router's WAN to the ONT's activated LAN port. Power-cycle both the ONT and router (off ~30s) and re-read the lights above. (The ONT may be inside, outside, or in a building's common comms room.)
2
Check the CPE & cabling. Note the router (CPE) make / model and its lights. Use RouteThis to check the cable between the router and the ONT. Confirm the activated LAN port in Anex → Service tab (the UNID Port field) and plug the router's WAN into that port. For migrated customers the UNID port in Anex may be wrong → try all 4 LAN ports. → LAN comes up → Step 3.
3
Isolation test. Connect a laptop/PC directly to the ONT's activated LAN port (bypassing the router) and restart the ONT. If that fails, test with the customer's PPPoE details (from Anex → Service tab) via a Windows Broadband (PPPoE) dial-up. → Works = the issue is the router / its config → Step 4. → Fails → Step 6.
4
Mandatory script — read before reset Advise the customer: “Please be aware that performing a factory reset on your modem/router will erase all customised settings. This includes but is not limited to WiFi details such as SSIDs, passwords, security settings, and channels, VoIP provisioning details for BYO devices, and any custom port forwarding settings. Do you understand and agree to proceed with the factory reset?”
Factory-reset & reconfigure (IPoE). Get consent, restart the ONT and router, then factory-reset the router and reconfigure it with IPoE (Dynamic IP) details (see BYO Modem Configuration). → IPoE works → confirm & end. → IPoE fails but the isolation test worked → try an alternate router / Ethernet cable, or offer an Origin device. → Both fail → Step 5.
5
Configure with PPPoE via ZTE ACS. Push the customer's PPPoE details to the modem through ZTE ACS (search by modem serial / MAC / IP → Internet Service One (PPPoE) → grab the PPPoE username & password from Anex). → PPPoE works → Step 6 (to find why IPoE failed). → PPPoE fails → revert the router to IPoE → Step 6.
6
Escalate to Level 2. Get the customer to reconnect the router to the ONT, then raise a Trouble Ticket to L2 to get the port reset done. Document every step and customer interaction in ANEX, and advise the customer of the escalation. (Tech-appointment and the $270 callout-fee script are below.)
No fault found? VPN on → ask them to turn it off. Splitters / filters or damaged cables → remove or replace, or have internal wiring checked. Wi-Fi only → check Wi-Fi settings and environment. Pages won't load → clear the browser cache / check DNS. Faulty, incompatible or outdated modem → offer an Origin-supplied device.
Escalation · L2 Reconnect the router to the ONT and raise a Trouble Ticket (correct fault type) to L2 to get the port reset; document everything in ANEX and advise the customer. If an OptiComm tech appointment is needed, capture three availability windows at least 2 business days out, and advise the customer that someone 18+ must be home and that the window isn't guaranteed. If no appointment is needed, document the justification in the ticket.
Mandatory script — $270 callout fee Before booking an OptiComm tech appointment, the customer must be told about the fee. A $270 callout fee applies if OptiComm find no fault in their network and trace the issue beyond their boundary (internal cabling, wiring, modem or other equipment). Advise the customer: “In the event that OptiComm cannot find any fault within their network, you may be charged a \$270 callout fee. Do you wish to proceed?” → Yes → proceed with the appointment. → No → revisit the steps and the No-fault-found points above.
Leave a note in ANEX at the end of every call — resolved or not.
Router configuration
Search for a customer's router to see how to set it up for an Origin service. As a rule, most routers need the internet connection set to Dynamic IP (IPoE) — no PPPoE username/password — with VLAN tagging off. Pick a model for the exact steps and a practice emulator. You’ll first confirm the NBN side is healthy (box lights + ANEX diagnostics) before any router steps appear.
5 routers available

Site editor & publishing

Customise the trainer without touching code. Reorder panes (drag the ⠿ grip), show or hide them, and edit headings and text. When you're happy, click Publish for the team — it downloads a fresh copy of this page with all your changes baked in. Put that file in your Teams/SharePoint folder; teammates Download it and open the local copy (opening it inside SharePoint won't work — the page is blocked there). Whenever you change something, Publish again and replace the shared file.

Page name
Shown in the top header bar and the browser tab. Leave blank to reset to the default.
Tab order — drag the ⠇ grip to rearrange the main tabs (Admin stays on the right)
⠇Learn the basics
⠇Tech types
⠇Router config
⠇Simulator
In-page edit mode
Turn on, then open any tab and click a heading or paragraph to edit it. Each pane gets ↑ ↓ Hide buttons.

Tip: editing rich text (the Learn page) keeps its formatting unless you delete it. If something looks off, use Reset.

Panes & headings
Rename a pane in the first box — type any case you like. The second box renames the heading shown on the page. Use the up / down arrows to reorder, and untick Show to hide a pane.
